title: Antidepressant use in spatial social networks
abstract: Social networks may help individuals maintain their mental health. Most empirical work based on small-scale surveys finds that cohesive social networks are critical for mental well-being, while diverse networks are considered less important. Here, we link data on antidepressant use of 277,344 small-town residents to a nationwide online social network. The data enable us to examine how individuals' mental health care is related to the spatial characteristics of their social networks including their ties in the local community and connections to distant communities. We find that, besides the cohesion of social networks around home, the diversity of connections to distant places is negatively correlated with the probability of antidepressant use. Spatial diversity of social networks is also associated with decreasing dosage in subsequent years. This relationship is independent from the local access to antidepressants and is more prevalent for young individuals. Structural features of spatial social networks are prospectively associated with depression treatment.
summary_en: Does your social network affect your mental health? Linking antidepressant-use data for 277,000 Hungarians in small towns to a nationwide online social network, this paper finds that social-network diversity — not just local cohesion — matters. People with social ties reaching distant communities are less likely to start antidepressants and more likely to decrease dosage over time. The effect is independent of local pharmacy access and strongest among young people. The paper challenges the conventional view that only close, cohesive networks support mental well-being — "bridging" ties to distant places matter too.
data_used: Hungarian prescription-drug (antidepressant) records for 277,344 residents of small Hungarian towns, linked to an anonymised nationwide online social network with measured geographic spread of ties; multi-year panel.
policy_relevance: For the Ministry of Interior mental-health portfolio and public-health agencies: supports place-based mental-health strategies that promote connectivity to other communities — e.g., through youth exchange programmes, migration-support, or regional mobility policies. Concrete evidence that geographic isolation has mental-health costs on top of traditional determinants. Hungarian data; immediately applicable. Caveat: correlational — causal direction is plausible but not definitively established.
